activities.name: Career Coaching Grant - IUI
activities.description: Indiana University has received over $1 million from Indiana's $25 million Career Coaching Grant, provided by the Indiana Commission for Higher Education. This funding supports the state's commitment to career coaching and navigation systems. Through IU's College + Career = Success program, the university will collaborate with 38 Indiana school districts to offer tailored career coaching and support services to students in Grades 6 to 12. The initiative, coordinated by the IU Office of School Partnerships, includes small group discussions, presentations about careers, and postsecondary education preparation, with involvement from students' families. Key figures such as Ken Iwama, IU vice president for regional campuses and online education, and Leah Nellis, IU Interim Assistant Vice President for School Partnerships, emphasize the program's impact on advancing socio-economic mobility and enhancing career guidance for first-generation students. This initiative aligns with the vision of Indiana Education Secretary Katie Jenner to integrate P-12 education, higher education, and the workforce.

activities.name: IU Student Success Corps (In Person)
activities.description:
The IU Office of Community Engagement leads a tutoring, mentoring, college/career readiness, and family engagement program. This is in collaboration with three other IU campuses (IU East, IU Southeast, IU South Bend). IU students provide in person tutoring and mentoring for K-12 students selected by partnering schools.

IU SSC is an equity program that focuses on students who will benefit from additional academic and social emotional support. The program is free to all students. IU students, who serve as tutors and mentors, are prepared to support culturally diverse students with diverse learning needs, including students with special needs.

activities.name: Lilly Epidemiology Fellowship
activities.description:
The Eli Lilly Enhanced Epidemiological Fellowship selects two students annually to be trained to take up the challenges of health outcomes research in the pharmaceutical industry.

The purpose of the fellowship program is to help develop and train the Fellows to be better prepared to take up the challenges of health outcomes research in the pharmaceutical industry. The program is being undertaken to introduce the Fellows to the various aspects of applying health outcomes in drug development through commercialization and develop their applied analytic skills. The program will serve to maintain and enhance a scientific link between Eli Lilly and Company and Indiana University Fairbanks School of Public Health scientists. Graduates of the program will also enhance the scientific link between Eli Lilly and Company and the Fairbanks School.

activities.name: iDEW: Computing by Design Summer Workshop
activities.description:
Through funding from the Indiana Department of Education, SoIC provided professional development to teachers who would likely be teaching computer science. Teachers possess a range of knowledge and experience - no computer science background to experienced teachers who are interested in offering a new approach to the subject.

Using iDEW’s curriculum, Computing by Design, we converted to online due to COVID (previous workshops were held on campus). Workshops included weekly synchronous meetings using Zoom, online resources and asynchronous tutorials, as well as an online community for teachers to engage with IUPUI faculty, staff, students and one another through Slack. After the summer training, monthly Zoom meetings are used to reconnect and address additional questions. SoIC uses these opportunities to learn about how they are implementing the material in their classes and how to improve the program for next year.

The Computing by Design Summer Workshop site, Computing by Design 2020 - CxD 2020 Summer Workshop (idew.org)          

As part of the training, we covered three CxD projects, each being offered as a full semester of study including the Trivia App, Chatbot and Internet of Things (IoT). We shipped Sparkfun Inventor’s Kits to each teacher for the IoT project. SparkFun Inventor's Kit for Photon - KIT-14684 - SparkFun Electronics

We contracted with PBLWorks to offer a full day of project-based learning training to teachers. PBLWorks

All online resources remain free and available to teachers.

This CxD Teacher Training Program will bring together a cohort of 75 high school teachers from across Indiana.  This program is being offered at no cost to participants, thanks to funding made possible by the Indiana Department of Education, and includes accommodations for those traveling outside a 25-mile radius of Indianapolis.

Computing by Design is a professional development opportunity for instructors eager to teach courses in computer science driven by project-based learning. The popular iDEW program has successfully implemented the CxD curriculum in teaching computer science at eight Indiana high schools.

Faculty from the Indiana University School of Informatics and Computing (SoIC) at IUPUI will teach the program courses, following a project-based learning methodology focused on developing lesson plans in computer science.
activities.units: Luddy School of Informatics, Computing, and Engineering

activities.name: Career Coaching Grant - IUI
activities.description: Indiana University has received over $1 million from Indiana's $25 million Career Coaching Grant, provided by the Indiana Commission for Higher Education. This funding supports the state's commitment to career coaching and navigation systems. Through IU's College + Career = Success program, the university will collaborate with 38 Indiana school districts to offer tailored career coaching and support services to students in Grades 6 to 12. The initiative, coordinated by the IU Office of School Partnerships, includes small group discussions, presentations about careers, and postsecondary education preparation, with involvement from students' families. Key figures such as Ken Iwama, IU vice president for regional campuses and online education, and Leah Nellis, IU Interim Assistant Vice President for School Partnerships, emphasize the program's impact on advancing socio-economic mobility and enhancing career guidance for first-generation students. This initiative aligns with the vision of Indiana Education Secretary Katie Jenner to integrate P-12 education, higher education, and the workforce.

activities.name: IUI Business Opportunities for Self Starters (BOSS) Summer Entrepreneurship Camp
activities.description:
BOSS Entrepreneurship Summer Camp is a 25-hour curriculum (l week) presented in Indianapolis Public Schools (IPS) high schools to teach high school students about entrepreneurship, business start-up, and owning a business as a career. During the week-long pilot program at the four IPS high schools in 2021 and 2022, students will draw from their own interests and business ideas to "create" individual businesses. Up to 20 students may be enrolled at each high school.  During the week-long camp students will conduct business research and be inspired by local entrepreneurs and business owners. At the end of the week, students will have learned about business types, funding, where and how to locate a business, advertising and promotion, management and personnel, manufacturing and many other important aspects of business ownership. Students will be introduced to community leaders, IUPUI Kelley School of Business alumni and professors, and various local small business owners and entrepreneurs. Students will create and practice elevator speeches to promote their idea and give informal presentations to a group of judges for prize money ranging from $50-$100 for a well considered plan. 

The overarching goals of this program are to encourage students to begin developing an entrepreneur's mind set and to offer insight into the various functions of business and how they could form a company as a career option. Not only do students learn how to start a business but also to promote their business ideas using critical-thinking and research skills that they will use to market themselves to future employers. This program is unique because unlike other summer camps, students get paid for their work and gain work experience such as team-building, verbal and written communication skills, and professionalism — all of which are fundamental for the success of their future endeavors.
activities.units: Herron School of Art and Design,Kelley School of Business
goal_names: IUI 2030 Strategic Plan Pillar 3, Goal 2: P-12 Education

activities.name: IU Student Success Corps (In Person)
activities.description:
The IU Office of Community Engagement leads a tutoring, mentoring, college/career readiness, and family engagement program. This is in collaboration with three other IU campuses (IU East, IU Southeast, IU South Bend). IU students provide in person tutoring and mentoring for K-12 students selected by partnering schools.

IU SSC is an equity program that focuses on students who will benefit from additional academic and social emotional support. The program is free to all students. IU students, who serve as tutors and mentors, are prepared to support culturally diverse students with diverse learning needs, including students with special needs.
activities.units: Family, School, and Neighborhood Engagement,Office of Workforce and Community Engagement
goal_names: IUI 2030 Strategic Plan Pillar 3, Goal 2: P-12 Education

activities.name: TRIO Upward Bound
activities.description:
Housed at IU Indianapolis, the Upward Bound program is designed to bridge the gap between high school and college. We'll help you prepare for college—from admissions to academics to campus life.

Program Objectives

  • Provide academic skill instruction in math, science, and language arts
  • Make available tutoring and academic counseling for all participants
  • Make a positive impact on test scores and grade point averages of students with a special emphasis on math, science, and language arts
  • Expose students to new academic, social, and cultural learning experiences
  • Enhance the overall personal and academic development of all participants
  • Instill a positive attitude and appreciation toward lifelong learning
  • Assist participants and parents/guardians with the preparation of college applications and financial aid forms

The Upward Bound enrollment process is highly selective. Program participants are expected to display a mature and positive demeanor. Each participant will be held accountable for maintaining a positive, productive, and respectful attitude and behavior.

To qualify, you must:

  • Be enrolled in the 9th or 10th grade
  • Have a GPA of at least a 2.5 on a 4.0 scale
  • Be a U.S. citizen or permanent resident
  • Meet federal income guidelines
  • Be a first-generation college student
  • Enroll in the Indiana Core 40 curriculum in high school
  • Have a strong desire to attend college
  • Complete the Upward Bound application process
  • Be enrolled in one of the following high schools:
    • Arsenal Technical
    • Crispus Attucks
    • George Washington
    • Shortridge
    • Warren Central

 

Summer Academy

During the summer, participants are offered the opportunity to live the college experience. Students take academic classes and live in residential housing on the IUPUI campus. Summer Academy exposes participants not only to challenges and fun but also offers students the chance to gain a sense of independence, improve social interaction skills, and most importantly, lessen the anxiety of their future college experience.

Benefits of Participating

  • Receive one-on-one mentoring and advising 
  • Engage in tutoring and study sessions 
  • Visit colleges and universities in the region 
  • Take classes that give you the foundation to succeed in college 
  • Socialize with other students and learn about different ethnic and cultural backgrounds 
  • Take trips to museums, philharmonic concerts, the theater, and more 
  • Volunteer in the community 
  • Take part in leadership development workshops and conferences 
  • Explore career opportunities 
  • Learn more about the college admissions and financial aid processes
activities.units: University College
goal_names: IUI 2030 Strategic Plan Pillar 3, Goal 2: P-12 Education

activities.name: SEAL Indiana
activities.description: SEAL INDIANA is a statewide dental program that provides preventive oral health services for children who do not have adequate access to dental care using portable equipment. The Indiana State Department of Health and Indiana University Purdue University at Indianapolis provided start-up funding for the program, which began in March 2003. SEAL INDIANA resources include four portable dental chairs and units and a passenger van to carry faculty, staff, students and equipment. Services provided by SEAL INDIANA include dental sealants, examinations, toothbrush prophies, oral hygiene instructions and fluoride varnish. Treatment is based on the individual needs of each child. SEAL INDIANA does not provide any restorative services. This is consistent with the philosophy that restorative dental treatment should be done in a stationary dental office and that children’s oral health needs are best served when there is continuity of care in a local dental office or clinic.
activities.units: Center for Service and Learning,IUSD: Department of Cariology, Operative Dentistry, and Dental Public Health,School of Dentistry

activities.name: Assessment of Health First Indiana Funding Impact and Program Achievements
activities.description:
In 2023, the Indiana General Assembly appropriated $225 million in new biennium public health funding for fiscal years 2024 and 2025 for the Health First Indiana (HFI) program. Under the HFI initiative, the state disperses funds to local health departments (LHDs), and localities use the HFI funding to provide core public health services. The state dispersed a total of $75 million in 2024 and will disperse $150
million in 2025.

Before the HFI initiative, counties received a total of about $7 million from the state annually. The HFI initiative marks a 1,500 percent increase in the state’s average annual investment in public health in 2024 and 2025.

This preliminary report estimates cost savings from three key service areas that LHDs have been able to provide and expand using HFI funds: (1) prenatal care, (2) blood pressure screenings, and (3) fall prevention services.
activities.units: Richard M. Fairbanks School of Public Health

activities.name: Scott County HIV Task Force
activities.description:
The Indiana State Department of Health and the Scott County Health Department, in conjunction with Dr. Duwve set up a community outreach center with testing, counseling, insurance enrollment, and care providers. Several infectious disease doctors from IU School of Medicine worked with the family doctor to start treating people with HIV. Churches offered transportation and meals. 

The Task Force worked with the state medical licensing board to develop rules and a toolkit for using best practices for prescriptions, to set realistic patient expectations, and to encourage use of alternative methods to address pain.
activities.units: Richard M. Fairbanks School of Public Health,School of Medicine

activities.name: Strengthening Indiana Families
activities.description: Strengthening Indiana Families (SIF) focuses on the implementation and evaluation of a continuum of community-based services and supports designed to improve the safety and well-being of families in East Central Indiana.  Intended to prevent formal entry of children into foster care, SIF is being carried out in collaboration with a number of community partners including the Indiana Department of Child Services, Indiana State Department of Health, Children’s Bureau, Inc., Prevent Child Abuse Indiana, Indiana Family and Social Services Administration, Commission on Improving the Status of Children in Indiana, and local municipalities and agencies.  Families and youth with experience in the foster care system are integral to SIF’s design and implementation. Since the Family Resource Centers opened in 2021, they have had over 15,000 visits to Susy's store, family fun events, parent cafes, A Window Between Worlds, collocated services, and other programming. See Facebook pages for Grant, Delaware, Madison, and Tipton Counties Family Resource Centers. The project is funded by a five-year, $2.84 million award from the Children’s Bureau, Administration on Children, Youth and Families, Administration for Children and Families, U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, under grant #90CA1864. The contents of this summary are solely the responsibility of the authors and do not necessarily represent the official views of the Children’s Bureau. For project updates see the Mid Project Report and YouTube channel

activities.name: Alcohol, Tobacco, and other Drug Policy Scan and Guidance
activities.description:
This project aims to analyze and compile policies related to alcohol, tobacco, and other drug (ATOD) use in Indiana to create a guide for public health workers. The goal is to help professionals understand and advocate for policy changes that protect public health and prevent substance use.

The guide will cover local, state, and federal policies, including those temporarily modified during the COVID-19 pandemic (e.g., carry-out and delivery services) and compare them with similar changes in other states. It will also provide resources for policy change efforts, such as model policy language.

The final guide will be professionally formatted, accessible under ADA and Section 508 standards, and ready for public dissemination.

activities.name: Social Needs Screening for Department of Corrections
activities.description: The Indiana Department of Correction (IDoC) is working to improve health outcomes and quality of care for incarcerated and recently released individuals by addressing their social determinants of health, such as housing, employment, and access to care. The goal is to better identify and manage social risk factors to support successful reintegration into society. To achieve this, FSPH faculty and staff are identifying key social health domains through research, creating interview guides, and conducting interviews with IDoC staff, community advocates, and recently released individuals. The project is developing a practical, evidence-based screening tool to enhance risk identification and linkage to supportive services.

activities.name: iDEW: Computing by Design Summer Workshop
activities.description:
Through funding from the Indiana Department of Education, SoIC provided professional development to teachers who would likely be teaching computer science. Teachers possess a range of knowledge and experience - no computer science background to experienced teachers who are interested in offering a new approach to the subject.

Using iDEW’s curriculum, Computing by Design, we converted to online due to COVID (previous workshops were held on campus). Workshops included weekly synchronous meetings using Zoom, online resources and asynchronous tutorials, as well as an online community for teachers to engage with IUPUI faculty, staff, students and one another through Slack. After the summer training, monthly Zoom meetings are used to reconnect and address additional questions. SoIC uses these opportunities to learn about how they are implementing the material in their classes and how to improve the program for next year.

The Computing by Design Summer Workshop site, Computing by Design 2020 - CxD 2020 Summer Workshop (idew.org)          

As part of the training, we covered three CxD projects, each being offered as a full semester of study including the Trivia App, Chatbot and Internet of Things (IoT). We shipped Sparkfun Inventor’s Kits to each teacher for the IoT project. SparkFun Inventor's Kit for Photon - KIT-14684 - SparkFun Electronics

We contracted with PBLWorks to offer a full day of project-based learning training to teachers. PBLWorks

All online resources remain free and available to teachers.

This CxD Teacher Training Program will bring together a cohort of 75 high school teachers from across Indiana.  This program is being offered at no cost to participants, thanks to funding made possible by the Indiana Department of Education, and includes accommodations for those traveling outside a 25-mile radius of Indianapolis.

Computing by Design is a professional development opportunity for instructors eager to teach courses in computer science driven by project-based learning. The popular iDEW program has successfully implemented the CxD curriculum in teaching computer science at eight Indiana high schools.

Faculty from the Indiana University School of Informatics and Computing (SoIC) at IUPUI will teach the program courses, following a project-based learning methodology focused on developing lesson plans in computer science.

entity.name: Indiana House Democratic Caucus
entity.description: Interns work alongside state representatives and staff at the Indiana Statehouse during a session of the Indiana General Assembly. Specific responsibilities vary by position, but all interns are expected to have excellent writing and communication skills, organizational skills, time management skills and a professional attitude. Departments seeking interns include Legislative and Constituent Services, Communications, Policy, Fiscal and Black Legislative Caucus. 1. For the upcoming 2023 Legislative Session internship, the program will begin with an orientation week in the last week of December 2022 and lasts through the first week of May 2023. Applications are due by 11:59 p.m. ET on October 31, 2022. Applications received by 11:59 p.m. ET on October 15, 2022, will receive priority consideration. 1. Interns are paid $800 biweekly before tax withholding. In addition, one Indiana House Democratic Caucus intern will be selected to receive a $3,000 scholarship sponsored by Verizon Communications, Inc.

activities.name: Diabetes Impact Project - Indianapolis Neighborhoods (DIP-IN)
activities.description: This project aims to enhance the quality of life for people living with diabetes by reducing complications, increasing access to care, and ensuring ongoing treatment. It seeks to raise community awareness about diabetes risk factors and encourage at-risk individuals to get screened and take proactive steps. Additionally, the project  works to create a supportive physical and social environment that promotes health and well-being for all residents. Finally, it builds community capacity by focusing on civic engagement and strengthening local leadership to effectively lead diabetes prevention initiatives.

activities.name: Healthy Minds, Happy Families/Mente Sana, Familia Feliz (HMHF)
activities.description: The Healthy Mind, Healthy Families (HMHF) grant supports a community partnership between the Latino Health Organization and Indiana University Indianapolis to improve mental health in Latino youth and families in Marion County. This work builds on a successful one-week teen summer camp focused on resilience and now expands to include a parent program and yearlong support. The grant funds recruitment, culturally tailored programming, data collection, and ongoing community engagement. By strengthening mental health and family bonds, this project addresses health disparities and builds evidence for effective, community-driven mental health strategies tailored to the needs of Latino families.

activities.name: iDEW: Computing by Design Summer Workshop
activities.description:
Through funding from the Indiana Department of Education, SoIC provided professional development to teachers who would likely be teaching computer science. Teachers possess a range of knowledge and experience - no computer science background to experienced teachers who are interested in offering a new approach to the subject.

Using iDEW’s curriculum, Computing by Design, we converted to online due to COVID (previous workshops were held on campus). Workshops included weekly synchronous meetings using Zoom, online resources and asynchronous tutorials, as well as an online community for teachers to engage with IUPUI faculty, staff, students and one another through Slack. After the summer training, monthly Zoom meetings are used to reconnect and address additional questions. SoIC uses these opportunities to learn about how they are implementing the material in their classes and how to improve the program for next year.

The Computing by Design Summer Workshop site, Computing by Design 2020 - CxD 2020 Summer Workshop (idew.org)          

As part of the training, we covered three CxD projects, each being offered as a full semester of study including the Trivia App, Chatbot and Internet of Things (IoT). We shipped Sparkfun Inventor’s Kits to each teacher for the IoT project. SparkFun Inventor's Kit for Photon - KIT-14684 - SparkFun Electronics

We contracted with PBLWorks to offer a full day of project-based learning training to teachers. PBLWorks

All online resources remain free and available to teachers.

This CxD Teacher Training Program will bring together a cohort of 75 high school teachers from across Indiana.  This program is being offered at no cost to participants, thanks to funding made possible by the Indiana Department of Education, and includes accommodations for those traveling outside a 25-mile radius of Indianapolis.

Computing by Design is a professional development opportunity for instructors eager to teach courses in computer science driven by project-based learning. The popular iDEW program has successfully implemented the CxD curriculum in teaching computer science at eight Indiana high schools.

Faculty from the Indiana University School of Informatics and Computing (SoIC) at IUPUI will teach the program courses, following a project-based learning methodology focused on developing lesson plans in computer science.
activities.units: Luddy School of Informatics, Computing, and Engineering
goal_names: IUI 2030 Strategic Plan Pillar 3, Goal 2: P-12 Education

activities.description:
The IU Office of Community Engagement leads a tutoring, mentoring, college/career readiness, and family engagement program. activities.name: Special Programs for Academic Nurturing (SPAN)
activities.description:
SPAN was established in 1984 to serve as the hub for all accelerated college immersion and early college entrance programming at the Indianapolis campus. The SPAN Division's mission and goal is to provide opportunities for gifted,high ability. and academically mmotivated high school students to enroll in college classes on the IUPUI campus. 

The SPAN Division has a long history of offering accelerated college immersion opportunities to students. Thousands of high school students have participated in our programs. Many SPAN students have remained at IUPUI to complete their college education and have received some of IUPUI’s top scholarships. Others have attended, or are presently attending, prestigious schools such as Harvard University, MIT, Stanford University, Yale University, and Cornell University.

Through SPAN, participants are able to:

  • Enroll in authentic college courses taught on the IUPUI campus
  • Graduate early from high school and college
  • Demonstrate academic prowess at a collegiate level
  • Discuss academic and career plans with a SPAN expert
  • Get to know other students interested in their particular career/professional fields
  • Earn dual credit*
  • Interact with knowledgeable faculty with world-class credentials
  • Explore more than 350 degree opportunities
  • Earn full university credit through an accelerated college immersion program that has a 30+ year success record and has enjoyed a 100% credit transfer rate to public colleges and universities across the nation and around the world.

*Dual credit is approved college coursework that may count toward high school graduation requirements. Participants should check with their local high school administration to confirm course eligibility prior to enrollment.

There are SIX programs in the SPAN Division:

The Running Start Program is open to high school freshmen and sophomores. Juniors and seniors who are not eligible for the Upper Class Program may be considered for the Running Start Program. Students are limited in total credit hours taken per semester and are restricted to 100-level courses.

Developed for top-ranked, high-achieving juniors and seniors, the Upper Class Program provides the opportunity for students to enroll in any course in which they qualify. Students may be eligible to enroll in as many as 18 college credit hours per semester. 

CyberTEENs Program: This program is designed for technologically gifted students who are interested in any of the computer-related degree programs at IUPUI, including computer information technology, media arts and sciences, informatics, and computer science. Students enrolled in this program may only take computer classes for which they qualify.

Computer Tech Scholars: The Department of Computer Information and Graphics Technology (CIT), part of the Purdue School of Engineering and Technology at IUPUI, partners with the SPAN Division to offer scholarships to qualified high school students interested in CIT courses. There are 13 CIT courses that students can take online at 50% of the normal tuition rate. Fees are not discounted.

Nanotechnology Program: Funded by the National Science Foundation, the NEST is a two-week summer camp program that provides a unique opportunity for high school students to explore the interdisciplinary field of nanotechnology at IUPUI. This program is open to students in grades 9–12 who are U.S. citizens or permanent residents. Students can earn 1 college credit hour in engineering and technology or science by doing hands-on laboratory experiences, conducting research on careers in nanotechnology, and connecting with a network of professors and scientists.

The Take6 Scholarship Program allows eligible students to enroll in up to 6 credit hours tuition-free. Eligibility requirements are as follows:

  • Applicants must be high-ability seniors who qualify for free or reduced lunch at their high schools or are 21st Century Scholars. Students with a cumulative GPA of 3.5 or higher have the strongest chance for admission.
  • Applicants must read the information guide below and submit the required application, recommendation forms, transcript, and essay by June 15 for fall admission or October 15 for spring admission. Earlier submissions are recommended to ensure availability of courses.
  • Students typically take one 3 credit hour class per semester and are responsible for mandatory fees, books, and travel expenses that are not covered by the scholarship.
activities.units: Special Programs for Academic Nurturing,University College
goal_names: IUI 2030 Strategic Plan Pillar 3, Goal 2: P-12 Education

activities.description: Dr. Max Jacobo Moreno, associate professor of environmental health science, recently presented "What data shows the future of water could look like," at TEDxIndianapolis. entity.name: Code Black Indy
entity.description:
CBI is a social enterprise and  501c3  tasked with creating a new standard of modern living by way of leveraging technology for cultural progression. 
 Our model of self sustainability provides training while setting students up for internal hiring as well as through CBI's B2B partnerships. We seek solutions with speed and efficiency.   
 By connecting and engaging with young people in interactive programming and education, we can change the face of innovative leaders in the growing tech industry in our city. We aim to create a powerful pipeline that incubates and fosters the success of black and brown young people from under served communities into the tech ecosystem in Indy.

activities.name: Career Coaching Grant - IUI
activities.description: Indiana University has received over $1 million from Indiana's $25 million Career Coaching Grant, provided by the Indiana Commission for Higher Education. This funding supports the state's commitment to career coaching and navigation systems. Through IU's College + Career = Success program, the university will collaborate with 38 Indiana school districts to offer tailored career coaching and support services to students in Grades 6 to 12. The initiative, coordinated by the IU Office of School Partnerships, includes small group discussions, presentations about careers, and postsecondary education preparation, with involvement from students' families. Key figures such as Ken Iwama, IU vice president for regional campuses and online education, and Leah Nellis, IU Interim Assistant Vice President for School Partnerships, emphasize the program's impact on advancing socio-economic mobility and enhancing career guidance for first-generation students. This initiative aligns with the vision of Indiana Education Secretary Katie Jenner to integrate P-12 education, higher education, and the workforce.
activities.units: Career Services
goal_names: IUI 2030 Strategic Plan Pillar 3, Goal 2: P-12 Education

activities.name: TRIO Upward Bound
activities.description:
Housed at IU Indianapolis, the Upward Bound program is designed to bridge the gap between high school and college. We'll help you prepare for college—from admissions to academics to campus life.

Program Objectives

  • Provide academic skill instruction in math, science, and language arts
  • Make available tutoring and academic counseling for all participants
  • Make a positive impact on test scores and grade point averages of students with a special emphasis on math, science, and language arts
  • Expose students to new academic, social, and cultural learning experiences
  • Enhance the overall personal and academic development of all participants
  • Instill a positive attitude and appreciation toward lifelong learning
  • Assist participants and parents/guardians with the preparation of college applications and financial aid forms

The Upward Bound enrollment process is highly selective. Program participants are expected to display a mature and positive demeanor. Each participant will be held accountable for maintaining a positive, productive, and respectful attitude and behavior.

To qualify, you must:

  • Be enrolled in the 9th or 10th grade
  • Have a GPA of at least a 2.5 on a 4.0 scale
  • Be a U.S. citizen or permanent resident
  • Meet federal income guidelines
  • Be a first-generation college student
  • Enroll in the Indiana Core 40 curriculum in high school
  • Have a strong desire to attend college
  • Complete the Upward Bound application process
  • Be enrolled in one of the following high schools:
    • Arsenal Technical
    • Crispus Attucks
    • George Washington
    • Shortridge
    • Warren Central

 

Summer Academy

During the summer, participants are offered the opportunity to live the college experience. Students take academic classes and live in residential housing on the IUPUI campus. Summer Academy exposes participants not only to challenges and fun but also offers students the chance to gain a sense of independence, improve social interaction skills, and most importantly, lessen the anxiety of their future college experience.

Benefits of Participating

  • Receive one-on-one mentoring and advising 
  • Engage in tutoring and study sessions 
  • Visit colleges and universities in the region 
  • Take classes that give you the foundation to succeed in college 
  • Socialize with other students and learn about different ethnic and cultural backgrounds 
  • Take trips to museums, philharmonic concerts, the theater, and more 
  • Volunteer in the community 
  • Take part in leadership development workshops and conferences 
  • Explore career opportunities 
  • Learn more about the college admissions and financial aid processes
activities.units: University College
goal_names: IUI 2030 Strategic Plan Pillar 3, Goal 2: P-12 Education

activities.name: Hepatitis C ECHO (Extension for Community Health Outcomes)
activities.description:
Project ECHO (Extension for Community Health Outcomes) is a movement to connect local primary care teams with interdisciplinary specialist teams to improve treatment for complex and chronic health conditions. The Project ECHO model provides training and mentorship to transform and sustain strong health systems where every person has fast access to the highest quality health care.

ECHO uses Zoom to facilitate virtual mentoring and knowledge sharing, enabling local primary care clinicians to provide best practice care for patients when they need it, close to home. ECHO provides a framework for sharing expertise across the state and, as a result, will help patients get access to care without leaving the providers they trust and the communities where they live and work via a teleclinic.

The Hepatitis C (HCV) ECHO helps connect primary care providers to teams of medical experts int he state to improve access to high-quality treatment for Hepatitis B & C.

As a member of HCV ECHO, you will gain the skills and confidence to treat patients with Hepatitis C in your community. The key to ECHO’s success is moving knowledge, not patients, providing the right care, in the right place, at the right time.
activities.units: Richard M. Fairbanks School of Public Health,School of Nursing
goal_names: IUI 2030 Strategic Plan Pillar 3, Goal 4: Health Service Outcomes

The library and museum serve as a cultural and educational resource unique to the nation.

activities.name: Central Indiana Wikipedia Partnership
activities.description: The Central Indiana Wikipedia Partnership is a group of organizations collaborating to improve the representation of Indiana’s heritage on Wikipedia. The Central Indiana Community Foundation’s (CICF) Library Fund has generously funded the partnership, whose main aim is to increase the capacity of central Indiana institutions to engage with Wikipedia by working together on programs such as staff training, contributing digital collection images by Wikimedia Commons, holding public editing events, and more.

activities.name: Copy of 13th Annual Martin Family Alzheimer’s Disease Caregiver Symposium
activities.description:
"Trains, Planes and Automobiles: Traveling and Dementia"

Friday, September 27, 2019 8:00 a.m. to 4:00 p.m.  
 
IU Health Neuroscience Center Goodman Hall Auditorium 355 W. 16th Street  Indianapolis, IN  46202 

The Indiana University School of Medicine’s Indiana Alzheimer Disease  Center (IADC) presents Trains, Planes and Automobiles: Traveling and Dementia to help caregivers be prepared to enjoy activities outside of the home for themselves and the person with dementia and to do so successfully. In addition, the program includes our annual research update and information on how to get involved.  

The 2019 caregiver symposium is designed to be beneficial for both family caregivers and professional care providers.

This year’s program includes:  

1. IADC research update  

2. Planning for successful holidays and travel

3. Making the difficult decision about driving

4. Avoiding social isolation due to lack of transportation

5. Panel presentations by both research participants and caregivers
activities.units: Indiana Alzheimer Disease Center,School of Social Work
goal_names: IUI 2030 Strategic Plan Pillar 3, Goal 4: Health Service Outcomes

activities.name: Extension for Community Healthcare Outcomes (ECHO): Cancer Prevention
activities.description: ECHO is a movement to connect local primary care teams with inter-disciplinary specialist teams to improve treatment for complex and chronic health conditions. ECHO uses technology to facilitate mentoring and knowledge sharing, enabling local primary care clinicians to provide best practice care for patients when they need it, close to home. ECHO provides a framework for sharing expertise across the state and as a result will help patients get access to care without leaving the providers they trust and the communities where they live and work via a teleclinic.
activities.units: Richard M. Fairbanks School of Public Health
goal_names: IUI 2030 Strategic Plan Pillar 3, Goal 4: Health Service Outcomes

activities.name: HIV ECHO (Extension for Community Healthcare Outcomes)
activities.description:
ECHO is a movement to connect local primary care teams with interdisciplinary specialist teams to improve treatment for complex and chronic health conditions. Project ECHO’s revolutionary model provides training and mentorship to transform and sustain strong health systems, where every person has fast access to the highest quality healthcare. 

ECHO utilizes video conferencing technology (Zoom) to facilitate mentoring and knowledge sharing, enabling local primary care clinicians to deliver best practice care to patients when needed, close to home. ECHO provides a framework for sharing expertise across the state and as a result will help patients get access to care without leaving the providers they trust and the communities where they live and work via a teleclinic.
activities.units: Richard M. Fairbanks School of Public Health
goal_names: IUI 2030 Strategic Plan Pillar 3, Goal 4: Health Service Outcomes

activities.name: Indiana Peer Education (INPEP) ECHO
activities.description:
Project ECHO (Extension for Community Health Outcomes) is a movement to connect local primary care teams with interdisciplinary specialist teams to improve treatment for complex and chronic health conditions. Project ECHO’s revolutionary model provides training and mentorship to transform and sustain strong health systems, where every person has fast access to the highest quality healthcare.

The Indiana Peer Education Program (INPEP) ECHO promotes health education within correctional facilities through peer-to-peer trainings and the Project ECHO model. The INPEP ECHO team equips people who are incarcerated as peer health educators, who then use their lived experience to teach health education workshops about unique health conditions affecting people who are incarcerated.
activities.units: Richard M. Fairbanks School of Public Health
goal_names: IUI 2030 Strategic Plan Pillar 3, Goal 4: Health Service Outcomes

Finally, it builds community capacity by focusing on civic engagement and strengthening local leadership to effectively lead diabetes prevention initiatives.

activities.name: Career Coaching Grant - IUI
activities.description: Indiana University has received over $1 million from Indiana's $25 million Career Coaching Grant, provided by the Indiana Commission for Higher Education. This funding supports the state's commitment to career coaching and navigation systems. Through IU's College + Career = Success program, the university will collaborate with 38 Indiana school districts to offer tailored career coaching and support services to students in Grades 6 to 12. The initiative, coordinated by the IU Office of School Partnerships, includes small group discussions, presentations about careers, and postsecondary education preparation, with involvement from students' families. Key figures such as Ken Iwama, IU vice president for regional campuses and online education, and Leah Nellis, IU Interim Assistant Vice President for School Partnerships, emphasize the program's impact on advancing socio-economic mobility and enhancing career guidance for first-generation students. This initiative aligns with the vision of Indiana Education Secretary Katie Jenner to integrate P-12 education, higher education, and the workforce.
activities.units: Career Services
goal_names: IUI 2030 Strategic Plan Pillar 3, Goal 2: P-12 Education

activities.name: Kids College
activities.description:
Kids College is a one- or two-week summer day camp offered by Indiana University for rising 5th—9th grade students. The camp is especially focused on welcoming and supporting students who might be the first in their family to go to college or are likely to qualify for the 21st Century Scholars program.

At Kids College, students will build confidence, learn more about who they are as learners, and explore future college and career paths. They’ll take part in fun, hands-on activities that help them see how college can help them reach their goals. The camp helps students understand how to join and succeed in programs like 21st Century Scholars—and how to take pride in their place in school, college, and the world.
activities.units: Herron School of Art and Design,Office of Workforce and Community Engagement,Richard M. Fairbanks School of Public Health,Robert H. McKinney School of Law,School of Nursing
goal_names: IUI 2030 Strategic Plan Pillar 3, Goal 2: P-12 Education

activities.name: Minority Engineering Advancement Program (MEAP)
activities.description:
MEAP is a week-long, summer, non-residential camp on the IUPUI campus. MEAP is for students who have completed 6th through 11th grade levels. The camp is designed for students that are underrepresented in engineering and technology careers.

MEAP Objectives:

To identify, recruit, and select minority students (grades 6-12) with demonstrated academic potential and to expose them to the fields of engineering and technology. 

To increase the students' awareness of career options available to engineers and technologists. 

To facilitate students' access to and interaction with positive role models from the fields of education and industry who will serve MEAP as workshop instructors, counselors, and mentors for students. 

To provide hands-on laboratory experiences and academic instruction similar to that of a typical first year of study in an engineering or technology curriculum. 

To demonstrate work-place environments by providing students with tours of local industries.
activities.units: School of Engineering and Technology
goal_names: IUI 2030 Strategic Plan Pillar 3, Goal 2: P-12 Education

activities.name: SEAL Indiana
activities.description: SEAL INDIANA is a statewide dental program that provides preventive oral health services for children who do not have adequate access to dental care using portable equipment. The Indiana State Department of Health and Indiana University Purdue University at Indianapolis provided start-up funding for the program, which began in March 2003. SEAL INDIANA resources include four portable dental chairs and units and a passenger van to carry faculty, staff, students and equipment. Services provided by SEAL INDIANA include dental sealants, examinations, toothbrush prophies, oral hygiene instructions and fluoride varnish. Treatment is based on the individual needs of each child. SEAL INDIANA does not provide any restorative services. This is consistent with the philosophy that restorative dental treatment should be done in a stationary dental office and that children’s oral health needs are best served when there is continuity of care in a local dental office or clinic.
activities.units: Center for Service and Learning,IUSD: Department of Cariology, Operative Dentistry, and Dental Public Health,School of Dentistry

activities.name: iDEW: Computing by Design Summer Workshop
activities.description:
Through funding from the Indiana Department of Education, SoIC provided professional development to teachers who would likely be teaching computer science. Teachers possess a range of knowledge and experience - no computer science background to experienced teachers who are interested in offering a new approach to the subject.

Using iDEW’s curriculum, Computing by Design, we converted to online due to COVID (previous workshops were held on campus). Workshops included weekly synchronous meetings using Zoom, online resources and asynchronous tutorials, as well as an online community for teachers to engage with IUPUI faculty, staff, students and one another through Slack. After the summer training, monthly Zoom meetings are used to reconnect and address additional questions. SoIC uses these opportunities to learn about how they are implementing the material in their classes and how to improve the program for next year.

The Computing by Design Summer Workshop site, Computing by Design 2020 - CxD 2020 Summer Workshop (idew.org)          

As part of the training, we covered three CxD projects, each being offered as a full semester of study including the Trivia App, Chatbot and Internet of Things (IoT). We shipped Sparkfun Inventor’s Kits to each teacher for the IoT project. SparkFun Inventor's Kit for Photon - KIT-14684 - SparkFun Electronics

We contracted with PBLWorks to offer a full day of project-based learning training to teachers. PBLWorks

All online resources remain free and available to teachers.

This CxD Teacher Training Program will bring together a cohort of 75 high school teachers from across Indiana.  This program is being offered at no cost to participants, thanks to funding made possible by the Indiana Department of Education, and includes accommodations for those traveling outside a 25-mile radius of Indianapolis.

Computing by Design is a professional development opportunity for instructors eager to teach courses in computer science driven by project-based learning. The popular iDEW program has successfully implemented the CxD curriculum in teaching computer science at eight Indiana high schools.

Faculty from the Indiana University School of Informatics and Computing (SoIC) at IUPUI will teach the program courses, following a project-based learning methodology focused on developing lesson plans in computer science.
activities.units: Luddy School of Informatics, Computing, and Engineering
goal_names: IUI 2030 Strategic Plan Pillar 3, Goal 2: P-12 Education

activities.name: IU Student Success Corps (In Person)
activities.description:
The IU Office of Community Engagement leads a tutoring, mentoring, college/career readiness, and family engagement program. This is in collaboration with three other IU campuses (IU East, IU Southeast, IU South Bend). IU students provide in person tutoring and mentoring for K-12 students selected by partnering schools.

IU SSC is an equity program that focuses on students who will benefit from additional academic and social emotional support. The program is free to all students. IU students, who serve as tutors and mentors, are prepared to support culturally diverse students with diverse learning needs, including students with special needs.

activities.name: Computable social factor phenotyping using EHR and HIE data
activities.description: This project seeks to improve how healthcare systems measure and address patients' health related social needs—such as housing, financial instability, and access to care—that impact health, costs, and care quality. Current approaches, like questionnaires and advanced text analysis (NLP), face accuracy and usability challenges. Researchers evaluate "computable social factor phenotypes," which use data from electronic health records (EHRs) and health information exchanges (HIEs), to identify health related social risks. The study assesses their accuracy, predictive value for healthcare costs and utilization, and reliability across diverse populations. This work aims to create practical tools for addressing social needs and improving care for various patient groups.
activities.units: Richard M. Fairbanks School of Public Health
goal_names: IUI 2030 Strategic Plan Pillar 3, Goal 4: Health Service Outcomes

activities.name: SEAL Indiana
activities.description: SEAL INDIANA is a statewide dental program that provides preventive oral health services for children who do not have adequate access to dental care using portable equipment. The Indiana State Department of Health and Indiana University Purdue University at Indianapolis provided start-up funding for the program, which began in March 2003. SEAL INDIANA resources include four portable dental chairs and units and a passenger van to carry faculty, staff, students and equipment. Services provided by SEAL INDIANA include dental sealants, examinations, toothbrush prophies, oral hygiene instructions and fluoride varnish. Treatment is based on the individual needs of each child. SEAL INDIANA does not provide any restorative services. This is consistent with the philosophy that restorative dental treatment should be done in a stationary dental office and that children’s oral health needs are best served when there is continuity of care in a local dental office or clinic.
activities.units: Center for Service and Learning,IUSD: Department of Cariology, Operative Dentistry, and Dental Public Health,School of Dentistry

programs: IUI 2030 Strategic Plan Pillar 3, Goal 4: Health Service Outcomes

activities.name: Computable social factor phenotyping using EHR and HIE data
activities.description: This project seeks to improve how healthcare systems measure and address patients' health related social needs—such as housing, financial instability, and access to care—that impact health, costs, and care quality. Current approaches, like questionnaires and advanced text analysis (NLP), face accuracy and usability challenges. Researchers evaluate "computable social factor phenotypes," which use data from electronic health records (EHRs) and health information exchanges (HIEs), to identify health related social risks. The study assesses their accuracy, predictive value for healthcare costs and utilization, and reliability across diverse populations. This work aims to create practical tools for addressing social needs and improving care for various patient groups.
activities.units: Richard M. Fairbanks School of Public Health
goal_names: IUI 2030 Strategic Plan Pillar 3, Goal 4: Health Service Outcomes

activities.name: Predictive modeling for social needs in emergency department settings
activities.description: This project explores how unmet social needs—such as housing, food, transportation, and access to care—affect health and healthcare use. These needs increase costs, emergency department (ED) visits, health disparities, and provider burnout. Researchers will compare two methods for identifying these needs: a predictive model using electronic health record (EHR) data and a 42-question survey. The study will evaluate which method works best and develop a clinical decision support (CDS) tool to improve referrals to social services. The research aims to reduce ED visits, enhance follow-up care, and improve screening for unmet social needs in diverse healthcare settings.
activities.units: Richard M. Fairbanks School of Public Health
goal_names: IUI 2030 Strategic Plan Pillar 3, Goal 4: Health Service Outcomes

activities.name: iDEW: Computing by Design Summer Workshop
activities.description:
Through funding from the Indiana Department of Education, SoIC provided professional development to teachers who would likely be teaching computer science. Teachers possess a range of knowledge and experience - no computer science background to experienced teachers who are interested in offering a new approach to the subject.

Using iDEW’s curriculum, Computing by Design, we converted to online due to COVID (previous workshops were held on campus). Workshops included weekly synchronous meetings using Zoom, online resources and asynchronous tutorials, as well as an online community for teachers to engage with IUPUI faculty, staff, students and one another through Slack. After the summer training, monthly Zoom meetings are used to reconnect and address additional questions. SoIC uses these opportunities to learn about how they are implementing the material in their classes and how to improve the program for next year.

The Computing by Design Summer Workshop site, Computing by Design 2020 - CxD 2020 Summer Workshop (idew.org)          

As part of the training, we covered three CxD projects, each being offered as a full semester of study including the Trivia App, Chatbot and Internet of Things (IoT). We shipped Sparkfun Inventor’s Kits to each teacher for the IoT project. SparkFun Inventor's Kit for Photon - KIT-14684 - SparkFun Electronics

We contracted with PBLWorks to offer a full day of project-based learning training to teachers. PBLWorks

All online resources remain free and available to teachers.

This CxD Teacher Training Program will bring together a cohort of 75 high school teachers from across Indiana.  This program is being offered at no cost to participants, thanks to funding made possible by the Indiana Department of Education, and includes accommodations for those traveling outside a 25-mile radius of Indianapolis.

Computing by Design is a professional development opportunity for instructors eager to teach courses in computer science driven by project-based learning. The popular iDEW program has successfully implemented the CxD curriculum in teaching computer science at eight Indiana high schools.

Faculty from the Indiana University School of Informatics and Computing (SoIC) at IUPUI will teach the program courses, following a project-based learning methodology focused on developing lesson plans in computer science.
activities.units: Luddy School of Informatics, Computing, and Engineering
goal_names: IUI 2030 Strategic Plan Pillar 3, Goal 2: P-12 Education

activities.name: Career Coaching Grant - IUI
activities.description: Indiana University has received over $1 million from Indiana's $25 million Career Coaching Grant, provided by the Indiana Commission for Higher Education. This funding supports the state's commitment to career coaching and navigation systems. Through IU's College + Career = Success program, the university will collaborate with 38 Indiana school districts to offer tailored career coaching and support services to students in Grades 6 to 12. The initiative, coordinated by the IU Office of School Partnerships, includes small group discussions, presentations about careers, and postsecondary education preparation, with involvement from students' families. Key figures such as Ken Iwama, IU vice president for regional campuses and online education, and Leah Nellis, IU Interim Assistant Vice President for School Partnerships, emphasize the program's impact on advancing socio-economic mobility and enhancing career guidance for first-generation students. This initiative aligns with the vision of Indiana Education Secretary Katie Jenner to integrate P-12 education, higher education, and the workforce.

activities.name: We the People: The Citizen and the Constitution
activities.description:
We the People: The Citizen and the Constitution is an instructional program for high, middle, and elementary school students which teaches the history and principles of the American constitutional democracy while enhancing students’ understanding of government. Students discover the contemporary relevance of the Constitution and the Bill of Rights at the same time. The program is based on materials developed by the Center for Civic Education and is nationally acclaimed by educators. We the People is aligned to the Indiana Academic Standards for Social Studies and English Language Arts.

The O'Neill School of Public and Environmental Affairs hosts the regional competition.

activities.name: Kids College
activities.description:
Kids College is a one- or two-week summer day camp offered by Indiana University for rising 5th—9th grade students. The camp is especially focused on welcoming and supporting students who might be the first in their family to go to college or are likely to qualify for the 21st Century Scholars program.

At Kids College, students will build confidence, learn more about who they are as learners, and explore future college and career paths. They’ll take part in fun, hands-on activities that help them see how college can help them reach their goals. The camp helps students understand how to join and succeed in programs like 21st Century Scholars—and how to take pride in their place in school, college, and the world.

activities.name: Special Programs for Academic Nurturing (SPAN)
activities.description:
SPAN was established in 1984 to serve as the hub for all accelerated college immersion and early college entrance programming at the Indianapolis campus. The SPAN Division's mission and goal is to provide opportunities for gifted,high ability. and academically mmotivated high school students to enroll in college classes on the IUPUI campus. 

The SPAN Division has a long history of offering accelerated college immersion opportunities to students. Thousands of high school students have participated in our programs. Many SPAN students have remained at IUPUI to complete their college education and have received some of IUPUI’s top scholarships. Others have attended, or are presently attending, prestigious schools such as Harvard University, MIT, Stanford University, Yale University, and Cornell University.

Through SPAN, participants are able to:

  • Enroll in authentic college courses taught on the IUPUI campus
  • Graduate early from high school and college
  • Demonstrate academic prowess at a collegiate level
  • Discuss academic and career plans with a SPAN expert
  • Get to know other students interested in their particular career/professional fields
  • Earn dual credit*
  • Interact with knowledgeable faculty with world-class credentials
  • Explore more than 350 degree opportunities
  • Earn full university credit through an accelerated college immersion program that has a 30+ year success record and has enjoyed a 100% credit transfer rate to public colleges and universities across the nation and around the world.

*Dual credit is approved college coursework that may count toward high school graduation requirements. Participants should check with their local high school administration to confirm course eligibility prior to enrollment.

There are SIX programs in the SPAN Division:

The Running Start Program is open to high school freshmen and sophomores. Juniors and seniors who are not eligible for the Upper Class Program may be considered for the Running Start Program. Students are limited in total credit hours taken per semester and are restricted to 100-level courses.

Developed for top-ranked, high-achieving juniors and seniors, the Upper Class Program provides the opportunity for students to enroll in any course in which they qualify. Students may be eligible to enroll in as many as 18 college credit hours per semester. 

CyberTEENs Program: This program is designed for technologically gifted students who are interested in any of the computer-related degree programs at IUPUI, including computer information technology, media arts and sciences, informatics, and computer science. Students enrolled in this program may only take computer classes for which they qualify.

Computer Tech Scholars: The Department of Computer Information and Graphics Technology (CIT), part of the Purdue School of Engineering and Technology at IUPUI, partners with the SPAN Division to offer scholarships to qualified high school students interested in CIT courses. There are 13 CIT courses that students can take online at 50% of the normal tuition rate. Fees are not discounted.

Nanotechnology Program: Funded by the National Science Foundation, the NEST is a two-week summer camp program that provides a unique opportunity for high school students to explore the interdisciplinary field of nanotechnology at IUPUI. This program is open to students in grades 9–12 who are U.S. citizens or permanent residents. Students can earn 1 college credit hour in engineering and technology or science by doing hands-on laboratory experiences, conducting research on careers in nanotechnology, and connecting with a network of professors and scientists.

The Take6 Scholarship Program allows eligible students to enroll in up to 6 credit hours tuition-free. Eligibility requirements are as follows:

  • Applicants must be high-ability seniors who qualify for free or reduced lunch at their high schools or are 21st Century Scholars. Students with a cumulative GPA of 3.5 or higher have the strongest chance for admission.
  • Applicants must read the information guide below and submit the required application, recommendation forms, transcript, and essay by June 15 for fall admission or October 15 for spring admission. Earlier submissions are recommended to ensure availability of courses.
  • Students typically take one 3 credit hour class per semester and are responsible for mandatory fees, books, and travel expenses that are not covered by the scholarship.
activities.units: Special Programs for Academic Nurturing,University College

activities.name: iDEW: Computing by Design Summer Workshop
activities.description:
Through funding from the Indiana Department of Education, SoIC provided professional development to teachers who would likely be teaching computer science. Teachers possess a range of knowledge and experience - no computer science background to experienced teachers who are interested in offering a new approach to the subject.

Using iDEW’s curriculum, Computing by Design, we converted to online due to COVID (previous workshops were held on campus). Workshops included weekly synchronous meetings using Zoom, online resources and asynchronous tutorials, as well as an online community for teachers to engage with IUPUI faculty, staff, students and one another through Slack. After the summer training, monthly Zoom meetings are used to reconnect and address additional questions. SoIC uses these opportunities to learn about how they are implementing the material in their classes and how to improve the program for next year.

The Computing by Design Summer Workshop site, Computing by Design 2020 - CxD 2020 Summer Workshop (idew.org)          

As part of the training, we covered three CxD projects, each being offered as a full semester of study including the Trivia App, Chatbot and Internet of Things (IoT). We shipped Sparkfun Inventor’s Kits to each teacher for the IoT project. SparkFun Inventor's Kit for Photon - KIT-14684 - SparkFun Electronics

We contracted with PBLWorks to offer a full day of project-based learning training to teachers. PBLWorks

All online resources remain free and available to teachers.

This CxD Teacher Training Program will bring together a cohort of 75 high school teachers from across Indiana.  This program is being offered at no cost to participants, thanks to funding made possible by the Indiana Department of Education, and includes accommodations for those traveling outside a 25-mile radius of Indianapolis.

Computing by Design is a professional development opportunity for instructors eager to teach courses in computer science driven by project-based learning. The popular iDEW program has successfully implemented the CxD curriculum in teaching computer science at eight Indiana high schools.

Faculty from the Indiana University School of Informatics and Computing (SoIC) at IUPUI will teach the program courses, following a project-based learning methodology focused on developing lesson plans in computer science.

activities.name: Special Programs for Academic Nurturing (SPAN)
activities.description:
SPAN was established in 1984 to serve as the hub for all accelerated college immersion and early college entrance programming at the Indianapolis campus. The SPAN Division's mission and goal is to provide opportunities for gifted,high ability. and academically mmotivated high school students to enroll in college classes on the IUPUI campus. 

The SPAN Division has a long history of offering accelerated college immersion opportunities to students. Thousands of high school students have participated in our programs. Many SPAN students have remained at IUPUI to complete their college education and have received some of IUPUI’s top scholarships. Others have attended, or are presently attending, prestigious schools such as Harvard University, MIT, Stanford University, Yale University, and Cornell University.

Through SPAN, participants are able to:

  • Enroll in authentic college courses taught on the IUPUI campus
  • Graduate early from high school and college
  • Demonstrate academic prowess at a collegiate level
  • Discuss academic and career plans with a SPAN expert
  • Get to know other students interested in their particular career/professional fields
  • Earn dual credit*
  • Interact with knowledgeable faculty with world-class credentials
  • Explore more than 350 degree opportunities
  • Earn full university credit through an accelerated college immersion program that has a 30+ year success record and has enjoyed a 100% credit transfer rate to public colleges and universities across the nation and around the world.

*Dual credit is approved college coursework that may count toward high school graduation requirements. Participants should check with their local high school administration to confirm course eligibility prior to enrollment.

There are SIX programs in the SPAN Division:

The Running Start Program is open to high school freshmen and sophomores. Juniors and seniors who are not eligible for the Upper Class Program may be considered for the Running Start Program. Students are limited in total credit hours taken per semester and are restricted to 100-level courses.

Developed for top-ranked, high-achieving juniors and seniors, the Upper Class Program provides the opportunity for students to enroll in any course in which they qualify. Students may be eligible to enroll in as many as 18 college credit hours per semester. 

CyberTEENs Program: This program is designed for technologically gifted students who are interested in any of the computer-related degree programs at IUPUI, including computer information technology, media arts and sciences, informatics, and computer science. Students enrolled in this program may only take computer classes for which they qualify.

Computer Tech Scholars: The Department of Computer Information and Graphics Technology (CIT), part of the Purdue School of Engineering and Technology at IUPUI, partners with the SPAN Division to offer scholarships to qualified high school students interested in CIT courses. There are 13 CIT courses that students can take online at 50% of the normal tuition rate. Fees are not discounted.

Nanotechnology Program: Funded by the National Science Foundation, the NEST is a two-week summer camp program that provides a unique opportunity for high school students to explore the interdisciplinary field of nanotechnology at IUPUI. This program is open to students in grades 9–12 who are U.S. citizens or permanent residents. Students can earn 1 college credit hour in engineering and technology or science by doing hands-on laboratory experiences, conducting research on careers in nanotechnology, and connecting with a network of professors and scientists.

The Take6 Scholarship Program allows eligible students to enroll in up to 6 credit hours tuition-free. Eligibility requirements are as follows:

  • Applicants must be high-ability seniors who qualify for free or reduced lunch at their high schools or are 21st Century Scholars. Students with a cumulative GPA of 3.5 or higher have the strongest chance for admission.
  • Applicants must read the information guide below and submit the required application, recommendation forms, transcript, and essay by June 15 for fall admission or October 15 for spring admission. Earlier submissions are recommended to ensure availability of courses.
  • Students typically take one 3 credit hour class per semester and are responsible for mandatory fees, books, and travel expenses that are not covered by the scholarship.

entity.name: Indianapolis Motor Speedway
entity.description:
The Indianapolis Motor Speedway is the home of the Indianapolis 500 and the Brickyard 400. It is located on the corner of 16th Street and Georgetown Road, approximately six miles west of Downtown Indianapolis.
Constructed in 1909, it is the original speedway, the first racing facility so named. It has a permanent seating capacity estimated at 235,000 with infield seating raising capacity to an approximate 400,000. It is the highest-capacity sports venue in the world.

activities.name: Strengthening Indiana Families
activities.description: Strengthening Indiana Families (SIF) focuses on the implementation and evaluation of a continuum of community-based services and supports designed to improve the safety and well-being of families in East Central Indiana.  Intended to prevent formal entry of children into foster care, SIF is being carried out in collaboration with a number of community partners including the Indiana Department of Child Services, Indiana State Department of Health, Children’s Bureau, Inc., Prevent Child Abuse Indiana, Indiana Family and Social Services Administration, Commission on Improving the Status of Children in Indiana, and local municipalities and agencies.  Families and youth with experience in the foster care system are integral to SIF’s design and implementation. Since the Family Resource Centers opened in 2021, they have had over 15,000 visits to Susy's store, family fun events, parent cafes, A Window Between Worlds, collocated services, and other programming. See Facebook pages for Grant, Delaware, Madison, and Tipton Counties Family Resource Centers. The project is funded by a five-year, $2.84 million award from the Children’s Bureau, Administration on Children, Youth and Families, Administration for Children and Families, U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, under grant #90CA1864. The contents of this summary are solely the responsibility of the authors and do not necessarily represent the official views of the Children’s Bureau. For project updates see the Mid Project Report and YouTube channel

activities.description:
The IU Office of Community Engagement leads a tutoring, mentoring, college/career readiness, and family engagement program. This is in collaboration with three other IU campuses (IU East, IU Southeast, IU South Bend). IU students provide in person tutoring and mentoring for K-12 students selected by partnering schools.

IU SSC is an equity program that focuses on students who will benefit from additional academic and social emotional support. The program is free to all students. IU students, who serve as tutors and mentors, are prepared to support culturally diverse students with diverse learning needs, including students with special needs.

activities.name: Predictive modeling for social needs in emergency department settings
activities.description: This project explores how unmet social needs—such as housing, food, transportation, and access to care—affect health and healthcare use. These needs increase costs, emergency department (ED) visits, health disparities, and provider burnout. Researchers will compare two methods for identifying these needs: a predictive model using electronic health record (EHR) data and a 42-question survey. The study will evaluate which method works best and develop a clinical decision support (CDS) tool to improve referrals to social services. The research aims to reduce ED visits, enhance follow-up care, and improve screening for unmet social needs in diverse healthcare settings.
activities.units: Richard M. Fairbanks School of Public Health
